levels.grammarNote: querer stem-changes e→ie: yo quiero, tú quieres, él quiere.
Me llamo literally means 'I call myself' — it uses the reflexive verb llamarse. More natural in conversation than 'mi nombre es'.
Subject pronouns are often dropped in Spanish because verb endings show who the subject is. 'Soy estudiante' and 'Yo soy estudiante' are both correct — use yo for emphasis.
Spanish has two 'you': tú (informal, one person) and usted (formal). With friends use tú. With strangers, elders, or in formal settings use usted, which takes the same verb form as él/ella.
Spanish usually drops 'it' entirely — verb endings and context carry the meaning. 'Es bueno' (it is good), 'Está aquí' (it is here). ello/lo exist but are rarely used in speech.
este (masc.) / esta (fem.) for 'this'. Neutral form: esto for unknown or abstract things — '¿Qué es esto?' (What is this?).
por qué (two words, accents) = why? / porque (one word, no accent) = because. ¿Por qué no vienes? — Porque estoy cansado.
Spanish greetings by time: buenos días (morning), buenas tardes (afternoon/evening), buenas noches (night). Casually, just buenas works at any hour.

levels.front: Good morning! How did you sleep?

levels.grammarNote: perdón = excuse me (apology or getting attention). con permiso = excuse me (when passing someone or leaving). disculpe is also common for getting a stranger's attention.

levels.front: Excuse me, where is the main street?
levels.literal: Excuse-me, where is the street main?

levels.grammarNote: poder stem-changes o→ue: yo puedo, tú puedes, él puede.

levels.front: I don't know where it is.

levels.grammarNote: tener is irregular in yo: tengo. Also used for physical states: tener hambre (to be hungry), tener sed (to be thirsty), tener frío/calor (to be cold/hot).

levels.front: I'm hungry. Do you have water?

levels.grammarNote: hacer is irregular in yo: hago. Used in weather: 'Hace calor' (It's hot), 'Hace frío' (It's cold).
gustar works in reverse: the thing liked is the grammatical subject. 'Me gusta el café' = coffee pleases me = I like coffee. Singular: me gusta. Plural: me gustan ('Me gustan los libros').

levels.front: I want to go, but I can't.

levels.grammarNote: y changes to e before words starting with i or hi: 'madre e hija' (mother and daughter). o changes to u before o or ho: 'siete u ocho' (seven or eight).
Spanish uses tener (to have) for hunger, thirst, and temperature: tengo hambre (I'm hungry), tengo sed (I'm thirsty), tengo frío/calor (I'm cold/hot). These are 'having' a feeling, not 'being' one.

levels.front: Do you know a good place?

levels.grammarNote: agua is feminine but uses 'el' in singular because it begins with a stressed 'a'. Adjectives still agree feminine: 'el agua fría'. Plural: las aguas.

levels.back: Un vaso de agua, por favor.

levels.grammarNote: Use estar (not ser) with food adjectives: 'La comida está deliciosa' (the food is [right now] delicious). ser would imply a permanent quality, which sounds odd for food.

levels.front: I always drink coffee in the morning.
levels.back: Siempre tomo café por la mañana.
levels.literal: [I] always drink coffee in the morning.

levels.grammarNote: nunca usually comes before the verb. If placed after, add no before the verb: 'no llego tarde nunca'. Unlike English, double negatives are grammatically correct in Spanish.
ir a + infinitive = going to do something: 'voy a comer' (I'm going to eat). Also: a + el contracts to al — 'voy al mercado' (not 'a el mercado').
venir is irregular: yo vengo, tú vienes, él viene. 'Venir de' = to come from: '¿De dónde vienes?' (Where are you from?).
entender stem-changes e→ie: entiendo, entiendes, entiende.
hablar is the model regular -ar verb: hablo, hablas, habla, hablamos, hablan. Most -ar verbs follow this pattern exactly.
dar is irregular in yo: doy. The command 'dame' (give me) is extremely common: 'Dame el menú' (Give me the menu).
ver is irregular in yo: veo. 'A ver' (let's see) is one of the most common filler expressions in Spanish.
para = purpose or destination: 'un regalo para ti' (a gift for you). por = cause, exchange, duration: 'gracias por tu ayuda' (thanks for your help), 'por dos horas' (for two hours).

levels.front: This is for you. Thanks for everything.
levels.back: Esto es para ti. Gracias por todo.

levels.front: The book is on the table.
levels.back: El libro está en la mesa.

levels.grammarNote: de is one of the most common Spanish words. de + el = del (contraction required): 'el libro del maestro' (the teacher's book). Used for origin, possession, and material.
bueno shortens to buen before masculine nouns: 'un buen día' (a good day). 'Buenas' alone is a casual greeting at any time of day.
malo shortens to mal before masculine nouns: 'un mal día' (a bad day). Also used in 'estar mal' (to feel bad, to be wrong).
grande shortens to gran before any noun (masc. or fem.): 'un gran hombre' (a great man), 'una gran idea' (a great idea). Gran often implies greatness, not just size.

levels.front: I have a lot of work.

levels.back: Un poco de agua, por favor.

levels.grammarNote: se dice uses the reflexive se + third-person — literally 'how does one say?' This impersonal se is everywhere in Spanish: 'se come bien aquí' (one eats well here / the food is good here).

levels.front: How do you say 'thank you' in Spanish?
Excuse me, can you help me find the station?
levels.back: Perdón, ¿puedes ayudarme a encontrar la estación?
levels.literal: Excuse-me, can [you] help-me to find the station?

levels.front: How much does this shirt cost?

levels.grammarNote: lo siento (I feel it) = I'm sorry. For minor bumps or getting attention, use perdón. lo siento / lo siento mucho is for genuine apologies.

levels.back: No hay problema, no te preocupes.
levels.literal: Not there-is problem, not yourself worry [you].

levels.grammarNote: ser = permanent or defining traits: identity, origin, profession, material, time. 'Soy americano' (I'm American), 'Es médico' (He's a doctor), 'Son las tres' (It's three o'clock). When in doubt, ask: is this a permanent fact about the subject?

levels.front: I'm American and she's a doctor.
levels.back: Soy americano y ella es médica.
levels.literal: [I] am American and she is doctor.

levels.grammarNote: estar = temporary states, emotions, location, and progressive tenses. 'Estoy cansado' (I'm tired), 'Está en casa' (She's at home), 'Estamos comiendo' (We're eating). Location always uses estar — even for permanent buildings.

levels.front: I'm tired and I'm at home.
levels.back: Estoy cansado y estoy en casa.
levels.literal: [I] am tired and [I] am at home.

levels.grammarNote: saber = to know facts or how to do something: '¿Sabes nadar?' (Do you know how to swim?). Irregular yo: sé. Not to be confused with conocer, which means to know people or places.

levels.front: Do you know what time it is?

levels.grammarNote: conocer = to know or be familiar with people and places. '¿Conoces a María?' (Do you know María?). Notice the personal 'a' before people. Irregular yo: conozco.

levels.front: Do you know a good restaurant here?

levels.grammarNote: decir is irregular: digo, dices, dice. Command form: 'dime' (tell me). Appears constantly in '¿Cómo se dice...?' and 'quiero decir' (I mean).
pensar stem-changes e→ie: yo pienso, tú piensas. 'Pienso que' and 'creo que' both mean 'I think that' — creo is slightly more common in everyday speech.
esperar means both 'to wait' and 'to hope'. 'Espera un momento' (wait a moment). 'Espero que sí' (I hope so). Context makes it clear which is meant.
salir is irregular in yo: salgo. 'Salir a' = to go out to: '¿Salimos a comer?' (Shall we go out to eat?). 'Salir de' = to leave from: 'Salgo del trabajo a las seis' (I leave work at six).

levels.front: At what time does the bus leave?
levels.literal: At what hour leaves the bus?

levels.grammarNote: volver stem-changes o→ue: vuelvo, vuelves, vuelve. 'volver a + infinitive' = to do something again: 'vuelvo a intentarlo' (I'll try again).

mañana has two meanings: tomorrow and morning. 'Hasta mañana' = see you tomorrow. 'Por la mañana' = in the morning. The article or context makes it clear: 'la mañana' = the morning, 'mañana' alone = tomorrow.
'Hasta luego' = goodbye (literally: until later). Luego can also mean 'then' in a sequence: 'Primero como, luego descanso' (First I eat, then I rest).

ya is one of those high-frequency words with several meanings: 'ya terminé' (I already finished), '¡ya voy!' (coming! / I'm on my way!), 'ya no' (not anymore), '¡ya!' (okay!/got it!). You'll hear it constantly.
porque (one word, no accent) = because. por qué (two words, accent) = why? '¿Por qué no vienes?' — 'Porque estoy cansado.' An easy trick: the question form has the accent.

levels.front: I can't come because I'm tired.
levels.back: No puedo venir porque estoy cansado.
levels.literal: [I] not can come because [I] am tired.

levels.grammarNote: Both tal vez and quizás mean 'maybe'. They can trigger the subjunctive if the uncertainty is strong: 'Tal vez venga' (Maybe he'll come — subjunctive) vs 'Tal vez viene' (Maybe he's coming — indicative, more certain).
'¡Claro!' or '¡Claro que sí!' = Of course! '¡Claro que no!' = Of course not! Claro is also an adjective meaning 'clear' or 'light-colored'.
'¿Qué tal?' is a casual greeting. Common responses: 'Bien', 'Todo bien', 'Más o menos' (so-so). It also asks about specific things: '¿Qué tal el café?' (How's the coffee?).
'¡Vamos!' = Let's go! / Come on! '¡Vámonos!' = Let's get out of here! (more emphatic, reflexive). Both are used interchangeably in casual speech.
'¿verdad?' is a universal tag question like 'right?': 'Está bueno, ¿verdad?' (It's good, right?). '¿no?' is even simpler and very common: 'Hace calor, ¿no?' (It's hot, isn't it?).

levels.front: Can you say it again, please?

levels.back: Una mesa para dos, por favor.
